title: 〜ところだ
description: 〜ところだ pins an action to a point in time. The form of the verb in front of ところ sets the moment: dictionary form = about to do it, 〜ている form = right in the middle of it, and た form = just finished it.

content.text: ところ literally means "place," but here it marks a place in time. Change the verb form before it and you slide along the timeline of an action — just before, during, or just after. The three sections below each pair one formula with an example.
Dictionary form plus ところだ means you are on the verge of doing something — it hasn't started yet.

content.japanese: これから出（で）かけるところです。
content.english: I'm just about to head out.

content.text: In the Middle Of (〜ている ＋ ところだ)
The 〜ている form plus ところだ means the action is underway right now — you're in the thick of it.

content.japanese: ちょうど夕食（ゆうしょく）を作（つく）っているところです。
content.english: I'm right in the middle of making dinner.

content.japanese: 今（いま）、宿題（しゅくだい）をしているところだ。
content.english: I'm in the middle of doing my homework now.

content.text: た form plus ところだ means the action wrapped up a moment ago — you finished it just now.

content.japanese: 今（いま）、駅（えき）に着（つ）いたところです。
content.english: I just arrived at the station.

content.text: 〜たところだ (just finished) and 〜たばかりだ (just did) overlap but differ: たところだ is strictly about the moment an action ends, so it needs a genuinely fresh event, while たばかりだ describes felt recency and tolerates more elapsed time (日本（にほん）に来（き）たばかり can mean "I came to Japan recently," even weeks ago).

examples.japanese: これから出（で）かけるところです。
examples.english: I'm just about to head out.

examples.japanese: ちょうど夕食（ゆうしょく）を作（つく）っているところです。
examples.english: I'm right in the middle of making dinner.

examples.japanese: 今（いま）、宿題（しゅくだい）をしているところだ。
examples.english: I'm in the middle of doing my homework now.

examples.japanese: 今（いま）、駅（えき）に着（つ）いたところです。
examples.english: I just arrived at the station.

headings.text: In the Middle Of (〜ている ＋ ところだ)
